snippet:
MyApp/
├── app/
│   ├── build.gradle.kts
│   └── src/
│       └── main/
│           ├── java/
│           ├── res/
│           └── AndroidManifest.xml
└── build.gradle.kts
question: What is the `app/` folder in this project layout?
choices: A temporary cache folder created by Android Studio
The main application module — holds the code, resources, and manifest for the app itself
A folder only used for automated tests
The compiled output (APK) after building

explanation.summary: `app/` is a Gradle module — the primary module that contains your actual application: its Kotlin/Java code, its resources, and its manifest. Larger projects can have multiple modules, but nearly every Android project starts with a single `app` module.
lineByLine: `app/build.gradle.kts` — module-level build config: dependencies, SDK versions, applicationId.
`app/src/main/java/` — your Kotlin source code lives here.
`app/src/main/res/` — all app resources: layouts, strings, images, colors.
`app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml` — declares the app's components to the OS.
explanation.tip: When someone says 'open the app module', they mean this folder. It's the unit Gradle builds into an installable APK or AAB.

snippet:
res/
├── drawable/
│   └── ic_logo.xml
├── layout/
│   └── activity_main.xml
├── mipmap/
│   └── ic_launcher.png
└── values/
    ├── strings.xml
    └── colors.xml
question: Why are resources split into subfolders like `drawable/`, `layout/`, `values/`, and `mipmap/` instead of one flat folder?
choices: It's just a naming convention with no functional purpose
Android's build tools require resources to be grouped by type so they can be compiled and referenced correctly
Each folder represents a different app screen
It's required only for apps that support multiple languages

explanation.summary: The `res/` folder uses a strict, type-based naming convention that Android's build tools depend on. Each subfolder tells the compiler what kind of resource it holds, which determines how it gets compiled and how you reference it in code.
lineByLine: `drawable/` — images and vector graphics used inside the app UI.
`layout/` — XML files describing screen layouts (View-based UI).
`mipmap/` — specifically for launcher icons, at multiple densities.
`values/` — non-visual resources: strings, colors, dimensions, styles.
explanation.tip: This isn't optional organization — Android Studio and the build system expect these exact folder names. Put an image in `layout/` by mistake and the build will fail or the resource won't be found.

question: If a designer asks you to change the welcome text from "Welcome back!" to "Good to see you!", which is true?
choices: You'd need to find and edit every Kotlin file that displays a welcome message

You only need to edit this one line in strings.xml — every part of the app that displays this text automatically shows the new value, because they all point to the same resource rather than containing their own copy of the text
Editing strings.xml has no effect until the R class is manually regenerated by hand
The change requires updating both strings.xml and the AndroidManifest.xml

explanation.summary: Kotlin code never contains hardcoded text directly — it holds a reference (like `R.string.welcome_message`) that points back to this XML file. This one level of indirection means the text lives in exactly one place, no matter how many screens display it.
lineByLine: `<string name="welcome_message">` — the single source of truth for this text.
Any Kotlin code displaying this text does so through a reference back to this resource, not a hardcoded copy.
Change the XML value once, and every reference across the entire app reflects the new text automatically after the next build.
explanation.tip: This is the core relationship to understand: resources and code are decoupled. Code depends on resources by reference, not by containing a copy — which is exactly why translators can swap in `values-fr/strings.xml` without touching a single line of Kotlin.

question: What should a developer do if they see this generated `R` class in their project?
choices: Edit it directly to add new resources faster
Delete it — it's leftover clutter from a previous build
Never edit it — it's auto-generated from the res/ folder and gets overwritten on every build
Move it into the java/ folder so it's easier to find

explanation.summary: The `R` class is generated automatically by the Android build tools every time you build the project. It's a direct mirror of whatever is in `res/` — add a string in XML, a matching constant appears in `R` after the next build.
lineByLine: Each nested class (`string`, `drawable`, `layout`, etc.) matches a resource type folder.
Each field (`app_name`, `welcome_message`) matches a resource name declared in XML.
The integer values are resource IDs used internally by the Android resource system.
Any manual edit is wiped out the next time the project builds.
explanation.tip: If `R.string.something` is 'missing' or shows a red squiggle in your IDE, the fix is almost never to edit `R` — it's to check that the resource is correctly declared in the matching XML file in `res/`.

question: What's the difference between the project-level `build.gradle.kts` and the module-level (app) `build.gradle.kts`?
choices: There is no difference — they're identical copies for redundancy
The project-level file configures settings shared across all modules; the module-level file configures that specific module's dependencies, SDK versions, and applicationId
The project-level file is optional and can be deleted
The module-level file only exists for testing purposes

explanation.summary: Gradle configuration is layered. The top-level `build.gradle.kts` applies settings across every module in the project (like which plugin versions are available). Each module then has its own `build.gradle.kts` for its specific needs.
lineByLine: Project-level `build.gradle.kts` — declares plugin versions shared across all modules.
`settings.gradle.kts` — lists which modules belong to this project (e.g., `include(":app")`).
`app/build.gradle.kts` — module-specific: `applicationId`, `minSdk`, `targetSdk`, dependencies for that module only.
explanation.tip: In a single-module app, this split can feel redundant, but it becomes essential in larger projects with multiple modules (e.g., `app`, `core`, `feature-login`) that each need their own dependencies but share common plugin versions.

question: What does `minSdk = 24` mean for this app?
choices: The app requires exactly Android API level 24 and no other version
The app will not install on devices running an Android version older than API level 24
The app was built using Android Studio version 24
The app can only run on the first 24 devices it's installed on

explanation.summary: `minSdk` sets the floor — the oldest Android API level the app supports. Devices running an OS version below this simply cannot install the app from the Play Store or via APK.
lineByLine: `applicationId` — the unique identifier for this app on a device and on the Play Store.
`compileSdk` — the API level used to compile the app (access to the latest APIs).
`minSdk = 24` — oldest supported Android version (API 24 = Android 7.0).
`targetSdk = 34` — the API level the app is designed and tested against; affects default OS behaviors.
explanation.tip: Raising `minSdk` shrinks your potential user base (older devices excluded) but reduces the need for compatibility code. This tradeoff is a common discussion point in real Android projects.

question: Why does `ic_launcher` live in `mipmap/` instead of `drawable/` alongside the other icons?
choices: It's a naming mistake — both should be in drawable/
mipmap/ is reserved specifically for the app's launcher icon, which the OS may render at a different resolution than the current screen density
mipmap/ files load faster than drawable/ files
mipmap/ is only used in debug builds

explanation.summary: `mipmap/` exists specifically for launcher icons because the OS sometimes displays them at a resolution different from the device's screen density — for example, in an app drawer that uses larger icons, or on a home screen launcher with custom scaling.
lineByLine: `drawable/ic_weather_sunny.xml` — a regular in-app image, always rendered at the device's screen density.
`mipmap/ic_launcher.webp` — the launcher icon, which mipmap tooling can supply at multiple resolutions for the OS to choose from as needed.
This distinction only matters for the launcher icon — everything else always belongs in `drawable/`.
explanation.tip: As a simple rule: if it's the app icon shown on the home screen and app drawer, it goes in `mipmap/`. Every other image or icon in your app's UI goes in `drawable/`.

question: What does this `settings.gradle.kts` file tell Gradle?
choices: The app's display name shown on the home screen
Which modules are part of this build: app, core-network, and feature-forecast
The list of dependencies the app needs to download
The minimum Android version supported

explanation.summary: `settings.gradle.kts` registers every module that participates in the build. Gradle only builds modules explicitly listed here with `include(...)`.
lineByLine: `rootProject.name` — the internal project name (not the app's display name shown to users).
`include(":app")` — registers the main app module.
`include(":core-network")` and `include(":feature-forecast")` — additional modules, common in larger, multi-module apps.
Each included module needs its own folder with a matching `build.gradle.kts`.
explanation.tip: Single-module apps only list `:app`. As projects grow, teams split code into multiple modules (for build speed and separation of concerns) — and every one of them must be registered here or Gradle won't see it.

question: A teammate says "add a new screen layout file." Which folder should it go in?
choices: res/values/
res/drawable/
res/layout/
java/ (alongside the Kotlin files)

explanation.summary: `res/layout/` holds XML files that describe View-based UI screens and reusable row/item layouts. Every screen or reusable UI chunk defined in XML belongs here.
lineByLine: `activity_main.xml` — likely the layout for the app's main screen.
`item_forecast_row.xml` — a reusable layout for a single row in a list (like one day's forecast).
Both are `.xml` files under `res/layout/`, referenced in code as `R.layout.activity_main` etc.
explanation.tip: Even in a Compose-first app, you may still see a few `res/layout/` files for legacy Views or specific system integrations. In a pure Compose app, this folder may be nearly empty — screens are defined as `@Composable` functions instead.

topic: colors.xml as single source of truth
question: The button, the app bar, and a chart's highlight color all use `brand_primary`. Marketing asks for a rebrand to a new shade of blue. What has to change?
choices: Every screen's Kotlin/Compose code must be updated individually to the new hex value
Only this one line in colors.xml — the button, app bar, and chart all reference the same named color, so updating its value here updates all of them at once
Nothing — colors.xml is only read once at install time and can't be changed afterward
Both colors.xml and every layout XML file must be updated together

explanation.summary: Just like `strings.xml`, `colors.xml` is a single source of truth that many unrelated parts of the app can point to. Screens don't each store their own hex value — they all reference the same named color resource.
lineByLine: `<color name="brand_primary">#1565C0</color>` — one definition.
The button, app bar, and chart each hold a reference to `brand_primary`, not a copy of `#1565C0`.
Changing the value here cascades to every place that references it, without touching any UI code.
explanation.tip: This is the same relationship as strings: define once in `res/values/`, reference everywhere. It's why professional Android apps rarely hardcode colors or text directly in UI code — doing so breaks this one-source-of-truth relationship and makes future changes far more error-prone.

snippet:
app/src/
├── main/
│   └── java/com/example/weathernow/
├── test/
│   └── java/com/example/weathernow/
│       └── ForecastCalculatorTest.kt
└── androidTest/
    └── java/com/example/weathernow/
        └── MainActivityTest.kt
question: What's the difference between the `test/` folder and the `androidTest/` folder?
choices: There is no difference — they're duplicated for backup purposes
test/ runs fast on your local JVM (no device needed); androidTest/ runs on a real device or emulator and can interact with the UI
test/ is for unit tests written by senior developers; androidTest/ is for everyone else
androidTest/ is deprecated and should never be used

explanation.summary: Android projects have two separate test source sets because they serve fundamentally different purposes and run in different environments.
lineByLine: `src/main/` — your actual app code.
`src/test/` — local unit tests, run directly on your development machine's JVM. Fast, no emulator needed. Good for testing plain logic (like `ForecastCalculatorTest`).
`src/androidTest/` — instrumented tests, run on a real device or emulator. Slower, but can test actual UI behavior (like `MainActivityTest` tapping a button).
explanation.tip: If a test needs Android framework classes (like `Activity` or `Context`) or needs to interact with the screen, it belongs in `androidTest/`. If it's testing plain Kotlin logic with no Android dependencies, it belongs in `test/` and will run much faster.

snippet:
res/
├── values/
│   └── colors.xml          <- default (light mode)
├── values-night/
│   └── colors.xml          <- used automatically in dark mode
└── drawable-hdpi/
    └── ic_logo.png         <- used automatically on hdpi screens
question: Why are there two `colors.xml` files in different folders, both defining the same color names?
choices: It's a mistake — duplicate files should be deleted
The `-night` and `-hdpi` suffixes are resource qualifiers; Android automatically picks the matching folder based on device configuration (dark mode, screen density, language, etc.)
One file is for phones and the other is only for tablets
Android randomly picks one of the two files at each app launch

explanation.summary: Resource qualifiers let you provide different versions of the same resource for different device configurations. Android automatically selects the best-matching folder at runtime — your code never needs to check 'is it dark mode?' manually for resource selection.
lineByLine: `values/colors.xml` — default colors, used when no qualifier matches.
`values-night/colors.xml` — same color names, different values, used automatically when the device is in dark mode.
`drawable-hdpi/ic_logo.png` — used automatically on high-density screens; other density folders (`-mdpi`, `-xhdpi`, etc.) can coexist.
Your code just references `R.color.brand_primary` — Android resolves which file's definition to use.
explanation.tip: This is one of the most powerful ideas in Android resource management: the same resource *name* can have many *implementations*, and the OS picks the right one for you. This pattern extends to languages too (`values-fr/strings.xml` for French).

snippet:
MyApp/
├── app/
│   ├── build/          <- NOT checked into git
│   └── src/            <- checked into git
└── build/              <- NOT checked into git
question: Why do the `build/` folders typically appear in `.gitignore` and never get committed to version control?
choices: They contain sensitive API keys that must be hidden
They're entirely generated output from compiling the project — regenerated automatically from src/ every time you build, so committing them is pointless and bloats the repo
GitHub doesn't allow folders named 'build'
They only exist temporarily during Android Studio installation

explanation.summary: The `build/` folder holds compiled output: `.class` files, generated `R` classes, merged manifests, and the final APK/AAB. None of it is source code — all of it can be regenerated by running a build against `src/`.
lineByLine: `app/build/` — module-specific build output (compiled code, generated resources, APKs).
Top-level `build/` — shared build cache and outputs.
Both are excluded via `.gitignore` because committing generated files creates merge conflicts, bloats repo size, and provides zero value — they're never hand-edited or reviewed.
explanation.tip: The rule of thumb across almost all software projects, not just Android: if a folder can be deleted and fully regenerated by running a build command, it doesn't belong in version control.

snippet:
app/src/main/java/
└── com/
    └── example/
        └── weathernow/
            ├── MainActivity.kt
            └── ForecastRepository.kt
question: Why is `MainActivity.kt` nested four folders deep instead of sitting directly in the `java/` folder?
choices: It's an Android Studio bug that creates extra folders
The folder path mirrors the Kotlin package declaration (`com.example.weathernow`) at the top of each file — this is required, not optional
Deeper nesting makes the app run faster
It's only nested this way in demo projects, not real ones

explanation.summary: In Kotlin (and Java), the folder structure under a source root must exactly match the package declaration. `com.example.weathernow` as a package name requires the folders `com/example/weathernow/`.
lineByLine: The package name `com.example.weathernow` typically comes from the app's applicationId, reversed-domain style.
Each dot in the package name becomes a folder: `com` → `example` → `weathernow`.
`MainActivity.kt` inside that folder starts with `package com.example.weathernow` at the top.
Moving the file to a different folder without updating the package statement (or vice versa) will break the build.
explanation.tip: This nesting can look intimidating at first, but Android Studio manages it automatically — right-click → New → Kotlin Class/File creates it in the correct location and writes the correct package line for you.
